claimed_contribution: The paper identifies feelings of personal control as an overlooked driver of brand extension reactions.
It proposes and tests a mechanism in which low control increases the need for structure, leading people to impose tighter mental brand boundaries and reject poor-fitting extensions.

It documents boundary conditions showing when the effect is mitigated (opportunities to assert structure) and when the pattern can reverse in competition (when the poor-fitting option is the best overall choice).
context: The research is set in brand extension evaluation, where consumers and managers judge whether new offerings fit a parent brand. Empirical scenarios include extensions such as Honda resorts versus go-kart centers and multi-brand extension sets across consumer goods, plus a competitive Honda versus Hyatt resort context.
design_and_data: The paper reports four experiments with control manipulations and brand extension judgments, plus pretests for manipulations and fit classifications. Study 1 uses student participants (N=59) and compares evaluations of one poor-fitting and one good-fitting Honda extension; Study 2 uses online adults from Mechanical Turk (N=195) evaluating multiple poor- and good-fitting extensions across brands. Study 3 recruits sales and marketing managers online (N=93) in a 2 x 2 design crossing control (low/high) with opportunity to assert structure (no/yes), then measures willingness to launch poor- and good-fitting Honda extensions. Study 4 uses online participants (N=148) in a competitive choice framing (Honda wins, Hyatt wins, equal) and tests how control changes perceived fit when the poor-fitting option is or is not the best choice.
main_findings: When personal control is low, participants report lower fit and lower consideration for poor-fitting brand extensions, while responses to good-fitting extensions are not significantly affected.
Perceived fit mediates the effect of control on evaluations for poor-fitting extensions in Studies 1 and 2.

Providing an alternative chance to impose structure attenuates rejection of poor-fitting extensions (including among managers), and in a competitive set where the poor-fitting option is framed as best, low-control participants inflate its perceived fit.
one_line_summary: Across four studies, the paper shows that threatened personal control increases the need for structure, which usually lowers acceptance of poor-fitting brand extensions unless structure is restored elsewhere or competitive pressure motivates fit rationalization.
relation_to_prior_work: extends brand extension fit research by showing that fit judgments vary with personal control and compensatory structure motives, not only with product or brand characteristics, and applies compensatory control theory to consumer and managerial brand-extension decisions.
research_gap: Prior brand extension research had identified many drivers of fit and evaluation but had not examined feelings of personal control as a determinant. The paper also argues that existing work did not explain fit reactions as part of a broader motive to maintain order and structure.
research_question: How do feelings of personal control influence perceived fit and evaluations of brand extensions, especially those with questionable fit? Do low-control states make people impose tighter brand boundaries because of a heightened need for structure, and when is this effect weakened or reversed?
